words: BYERS SANDERSON: - Have to get back where the snow was to hear my feet crunch
in the snow. But now, it's a problem.
SAM SCHRAGER: Did you get overseas during World War I?

speaker: BS
words: No, I was on the Gulf of Mexico. Our main camp was Pensacola -and

speaker: BS
words: what was the name of this othere place? Well, it was out from Gulf

speaker: BS
words: Port there. Out at the Exposition buildings; our headquarters was

speaker: BS
words: there. There was a big airfield there and then Pensacola was the

speaker: BS
words: principal airport.

speaker: SS
words: Did you enlist, or did you get drafted?

speaker: BS
words: I was enlisted. I was a mechanic on the airplane. Some difference

speaker: BS
words: between them airplanes now and then. You could hear them old crates

speaker: BS
words: whistle as they. come down. Regular kites.

speaker: BS
words: What did you do as a mechanic on them? Was there much you could do

speaker: BS
words: to make 'em run?

speaker: BS
words: Yes, you worked on the Liberty motor and the Hispano. Hispano was

speaker: BS
words: a French motor; and the liberty motor and then they had another motor:

speaker: BS
words: I forget the name. Hall Scott I believe it was. But it was

speaker: BS
words: some difference than what it is today. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: They were made out of spruce, weren't they?

speaker: BS
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Werenfct they made out of spruce: part of those old planes?

speaker: BS
words: Yes, they was made outt "wood and they had wire struts, you know

speaker: BS
words: coming down the side and wood, and they was-- Now, you know the Potlatch—

speaker: BS
words: well, the government reserved all the spruce there was in

speaker: BS
words: the country here. Had every spruce tree in the country marked. They

speaker: BS
words: didn't last long, though. 'Course, the spruce, if they'd have a

speaker: BS
words: crash landing or anything, it'd splinter instead of cracking up.

speaker: BS
words: They'd bend, and they'd break alright, but they wouldn't pull apart.

speaker: SS
words: Well, I've heart that that had something to do with the timber pro

speaker: SS
words: duction going on the woods right then.

speaker: BS
words: It did.

speaker: SS
words: They were really pushing to get the spruce out.

speaker: BS
words: Yes, they was getting the spruce out; picking out choice stuff. The

speaker: BS
words: airplanes, they was principally made of all spruce. That's what I

speaker: BS
words: say, they was regular kites. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: Listen, I want to ask you about— some more about Bill Deary. You

speaker: SS
words: mentioned to mepiat he was a pretty good salesman.

speaker: SS
words: BS' Yes.

speaker: SS
words: What's the story on that? He actually did some selling himself?

speaker: BS
words: OH, yes. He sent his— well, I don't know whether it was his nephew-

speaker: BS
words: I think it was his nephew— he sent him back East to sell white pine

speaker: BS
words: and white fir. And he went back there and he didn't get a half a

speaker: BS
words: dozen orders. Well, the mill was gonna eithershut down or they was

speaker: BS
words: gonna get something. Old Bill he fired him, put him on another jobhe

speaker: BS
words: didn't fire him— he put him on another job and says, "I'll go back

speaker: BS
words: myself." He went back and called it silver pine. And he put it out

speaker: BS
words: 'tween eighteen, twenty dollars a thousand, and he just sold car load

speaker: BS
words: after carload, and kept the mill running through them hard times and

speaker: BS
words: come out with it. And made money.

speaker: SS
words: Those Eastern dudes didn't know what silver pine was,or they thought

speaker: SS
words: it was something fancy?

speaker: BS
words: No, they didn't know what silver pine was. They thought it was an

speaker: BS
words: other valuable wood. Well, it was, and it is. You can take and build

speaker: BS
words: a house with white fir and it's the best building material you can

speaker: BS
words: get, right today. For the inside work on a house, you know. It'll

speaker: BS
words: hold up. Good boards, it won't crack like the white pine and other

speaker: BS
words: stuff. 'Course, now they've got preservatives that they put on their

speaker: BS
words: lumber and it preserves it, but then, why, white fir was cheap and

speaker: BS
words: they could build the inside and the outside of the house and then cov

speaker: BS
words: er it, and they had as good building material those days as they

speaker: BS
words: could get.

speaker: SS
words: Did you say that he didn't get along too well with Featherstone? Was

speaker: SS
words: he the first superintendent?

speaker: BS
words: Yes, he was the first superintendent the Potlatch ever had in here,

speaker: BS
words: and he'd get his men to working, and Old Bill'd come up— at that

speaker: BS
words: time he traveled mostly by, what they called a hack, a buggy, team.

speaker: BS
words: And he'd come up and change his men all around. He'd have his men

speaker: BS
words: working doing such a thing, and Featherstone was kind of an indepen

speaker: BS
words: dent old codger anyhow, and he said, "Bill," he says, "why do you al

speaker: BS
words: ways come up and change my men around for?" He says, "I've got 'em

speaker: BS
words: doing as I want 'em to do," he says, "you come up here and you wreck

speaker: BS
words: my whole plarij"." Bill says, "I want 'em that way." Made Old Frank

speaker: BS
words: mad and he says, "Well," he says, "maybe you better take the whole

speaker: BS
words: works and run it." "Well," Bill says, "I can do that, too." He was

speaker: BS
words: a hard-boiled old customer. And Featherstone quit and Old Bill went

speaker: BS
words: to Spokane and he met T.P. Jones coming down the street, and he told

speaker: BS
words: T.P.,'you're just the man I'm alooking for. He says, I want you to

speaker: BS
words: come and be the superintendent of the Potlatch."

speaker: SS
words: Did he know who T. P. Jones — did he know T.P. Jones?

speaker: BS
words: Yeah, he knew him back in Cloquet, Minnesota. Jones was a railroad

speaker: BS
words: engineer and heworked in the camps.

speaker: SS
words: So what was Jones doing up here in Spokane?

speaker: SS
words: SANDERSON

speaker: BS
words: Well, he was hunting for work. And when they shut down and stuff up

speaker: BS
words: there, he just come West, you know. And Jones didn't want it at first.

speaker: BS
words: And finally, Old Deary talked him into it. Well, he come down and

speaker: BS
words: took over. And he was a wonderful old fellow to work for. He'd argue

speaker: BS
words: with you, but if you convinced him you was right, why, he'd give in

speaker: BS
words: to you. Most of them fellows won't give into you, you know, whether

speaker: BS
words: they're right or wrong. They want it as they want it, but T.P. wasn't

speaker: BS
words: that way. Now, like the men working at the camps; up here at Camp 8,

speaker: BS
words: that was the first driving outfit that they had, in here before the

speaker: BS
words: railroad got here. Well, he told the men they could have a saw, cross

speaker: BS
words: cut saw, a shovel and splitting maul and a couple of wedges and an

speaker: BS
words: axe. "But," he says, "you take anymore, I'll figure that you're steal

speaker: BS
words: ing." And, he says, "I'll handle it as such." Well, everybody had

speaker: BS
words: a saw and a snowshovel and a spade and axe. And they'd even haul wood

speaker: BS
words: in, load it on the train and roll it off at their places. They had

speaker: BS
words: houses along the track at Slabtown there, and they'd stop the train,

speaker: BS
words: roll off two, three logs along for each one and they'd saw 'em up.

speaker: SS
words: Was that OK by T.P.?

speaker: BS
words: Oh, yes. Yes, they'd cut those old white pine, you know, dead, old

speaker: BS
words: dead tamarack; they never used any of that wood at that time.

speaker: SS
words: When did these families move into Slabtown? HftO Loll'n

speaker: BS
words: They moved in there- that'd go back into 1905, '06, they settled up

speaker: BS
words: in here. At 1904, I think, they started to work on the dam before

speaker: BS
words: the railroad ever thought of getting in here, and they made the dam

speaker: BS
words: up here in 1904, I believe it was. Old Tilley Pelton—

speaker: SS
words: Tilley was working for Potlatch then?

speaker: BS
words: Oh, yes. Yeah, it was all Potlatch then.

speaker: SS
words: These first men in there at Collins and Slabtown, now, did you say

speaker: SS
words: that the Fries were in there right early?

speaker: BS
words: Yes. They was in there in the '90's. And that was the outpost for

speaker: BS
words: the mails being delivered. Old CoFin that lives down here, out from

speaker: BS
words: Pullman there, was the mail— he was fourteen years old and driving

speaker: BS
words: mail truck.

speaker: SS
words: Yes, I spoke to him. Well, Frei, now— did you say that he was inde

speaker: SS
words: pendent at first and he had his own sawmill— he wasn't part of Pot

speaker: SS
words: latch when he was ?

speaker: BS
words: No, no. He was never part ofPotlatch. He had alittle store and

speaker: BS
words: the post office and a couple, three buildings there at— oh, what was

speaker: BS
words: the name of that place? He called it? And he run the store, and there

speaker: BS
words: was a schoolhouse there. And then Old Abe Frei, he had a homestead

speaker: BS
words: and Jake Frei had a homestead; John Frei and them, they all had home

speaker: BS
words: steads right around Collins there. And they'd come up and turn around

speaker: BS
words: there with the mail and go on back down to Troy. They came out from

speaker: BS
words: Troy. The railroad come to Troy, you see, and they'd get their mail.

speaker: BS
words: And Old Frei, he sold out. He sold his— he's the one that made

speaker: BS
words: the money at that time— he sold his homestead for ten thousand dol

speaker: BS
words: lars. He come out smelling like a rose that time!

speaker: SS
words: I guess. He sold to somebody else?

speaker: BS
words: He sold to the Potlatch.

speaker: SS
words: They must have wanted it pretty bad to pay that.

speaker: BS
words: Why, he had timber on there— white pine was four feet through! Just

speaker: BS
words: like hair on a dog. And then Sam sold his later— sold his home

speaker: BS
words: stead— he didn't sell 'em the homestead, but he sold 'em the timber

speaker: BS
words: off of it and he got four dollars a thousand for cutting it and haul

speaker: BS
words: ing it and loading it down to the railroad.

speaker: SS
words: He didn't make out so good as his brother did, eh?

speaker: BS
words: No. But he made money at that. You could really do something with

speaker: BS
words: money at that time.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of people were Freis? Were they a real independent bunch

speaker: SS
words: of men? Sounds like they weren't too anxious to go to work for the

speaker: SS
words: company.

speaker: BS
words: No. No. They didn't work for the company at all. They was an indep

speaker: BS
words: endent outfit. 'Course, Old Sam raised cattle. Not a big amount,

speaker: BS
words: but he had cattle and he had his horses and he done farming and

speaker: BS
words: he had his store. And they made a good living. One of the girls— not

speaker: BS
words: the girls— but his wife taught school. So they had no worries at

speaker: BS
words: that time. And then when the railroad come through they picked up

speaker: BS
words: quite a lot of work from the railroad, hauling freight and stuff that

speaker: BS
words: way. Had a regular freight outfit hauling in.

speaker: SS
words: Was there actually much of a settlement at Collins, of homes?

speaker: BS
words: Well, yes. They was, I don't remember how many people there, but they

speaker: BS
words: was the Freis; Jake Frei and his outfit, and then they was Abe Frei,

speaker: BS
words: Old John Frei and then they had the school in the store, and it was

speaker: BS
words: an outpost.

speaker: SS
words: Sounds like a family town.

speaker: BS
words: Yeah, it was, good deal. But there was lots of outsiders that come

speaker: BS
words: in there.

speaker: SS
words: About when Potlatch came in, or before?

speaker: BS
words: Well, yeah, before the Potlatch come in. And after the railroad got

speaker: BS
words: in and everything, why, they'd stop and put their freight off.at Col

speaker: BS
words: lins. But, the Freis, they was pretty ambitious. There was nothing

speaker: BS
words: lazy about em at all. They was workers, but they was independent.

speaker: BS
words: And then, when they left here, they went to Moscow and got that old

speaker: BS
words: White Hotel. There on the corner at the intersection down there by

speaker: BS
words: the service station. And they run that, well, I guess they still own

speaker: BS
words: it.

speaker: SS
words: Huh. Are there any of the old-timers left of the Freis?

speaker: BS
words: Yeah. Yes, I think the girls are all alive. I run into 'em every

speaker: BS
words: little while, there in Moscow.

speaker: SS
words: What about Slabtown? When did that come in?

speaker: BS
words: Well, I think Slabtown was built in, oh,— 1904 or '05. You see, Sam

speaker: BS
words: put in a little sawmill there, and that's where it got it's name. Slabtown.

speaker: BS
words: And he built a store and a post office, and then they had about

speaker: BS
words: a dozen houses there. And Old Dave Gentry, he was always a cattleman,

speaker: BS
words: but Dave worked for the Potlatch when the Potlatch come in, and he

speaker: BS
words: bought and sold cattle on the side. He had a cabin across the river

speaker: BS
words: from Slabtown. They used to have quite a time there at Slabtown,

speaker: BS
words: they'd go out and lasso the steers and ride 'em bareback!! They always

speaker: BS
words: had a rope tied onto 'em so the steers couldn t gore em when

speaker: BS
words: they— As a kid I would go out there and we'd ride 'em and get buck

speaker: BS
words: ed off, why, sometimes they had two, three men on the ropes to hold

speaker: BS
words: the steers from going in and horning 'em. They'd bawl. They had a

speaker: BS
words: regular Jfbundup there every weekend. Old Dave Gentry, why, he—

speaker: BS
words: it'd just tickle him to death, you know. He | a jolly old fellow,

speaker: BS
words: anyhow, you know. They'd put up prizes for the best riders.

speaker: SS
words: Rodeo.

speaker: BS
words: Oh, yes. It wasn't only just Camp 8 affair. And then at the dam,

speaker: BS
words: the lumberjacks'd take and put on a birling contest.

speaker: SS
words: Yes, you were telling me about that.

speaker: BS
words: Yes, they'd get out there—

speaker: SS
words: Was Slabtown right next to Collins, just about?

speaker: BS
words: Oh, no. Slabtown was only about two and a half miles up here. Collins is four

speaker: BS
words: miles.

speaker: SS
words: Were they real different from Bovill as towns?

speaker: BS
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: SS
words: They were just small settlements?

speaker: BS
words: Small settlements. When Collins was in there, Bovill was just one or

speaker: BS
words: two little houses down here. Old Grandma Warren, she had a couple a

speaker: BS
words: shacks here. She had three log cabins, and then she sold out to the

speaker: BS
words: Hughey Bovill, and he was gonna make a, well— they had cayuses for

speaker: BS
words: traveling— sightseeing outfit.

speaker: SS
words: A resort? Was that what he had in mind?

speaker: BS
words: Yes, that's what he had in mind. Then the Potlatch come in and they

speaker: BS
words: wanted to buy the meadows to put their mill, instead of at Elk River.

speaker: BS
words: Later on, you know, they put it in at Elk River. But Old Bovill

speaker: BS
words: wanted so much for the meadows at that time— he was gonna make his

speaker: BS
words: fortune there— he wanted so much that Old Bill Deary says, "We'll

speaker: BS
words: just go on up to Elk River." 'Course, Elk River wasn't any town,

speaker: BS
words: but there was a good meadow there and his cruisers had told him that

speaker: BS
words: there was a good meadow up there and lots of water, and so, they put

speaker: BS
words: the Elk Rver mill in. And at that time, they put in what was sup

speaker: BS
words: posed to be the best mill in the world. All electrical. 'Course,

speaker: BS
words: they made their own juice and everything. And it was a wonderful

speaker: BS
words: mill, after it got to going, they had around two thousand, twentyfour

speaker: BS
words: hundred people, working there, -fhen they had all the logging

speaker: BS
words: camps out there. Logging camps was all donkeys at that time. Then

speaker: BS
words: later on they turned to electric donkeys. They had steam at first,

speaker: BS
words: then they converted 'em, put 'em into electric.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember the Bovills?

speaker: BS
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: What were they like?

speaker: BS
words: Oh, they was a real accommodating old couple. They was English. And

speaker: BS
words: they had that accent of the English. And they held ' selves alott.

speaker: BS
words: (aloof) 'Course, the Englishman does that, holds himself aloft. Now,

speaker: BS
words: Henry Mallory, he was going with one of the girls, and one night they

speaker: BS
words: had trouble down at one of these old shops where the ladies were; hook

speaker: BS
words: shops, as they called 'em, and he went down to settle the trouble.

speaker: BS
words: Well, the girl heard that Henry was down there, and boy, she put the

speaker: BS
words: skids on him right now!! And the Featherstones was just as independ -

speaker: BS
words: ent as they were—

speaker: SS
words: He was a Featherstone?

speaker: BS
words: Yeah, Old Henry was a Featherstone. After Frank quit Deary, why, he

speaker: BS
words: took over the post office in Bovill here. He handled the post office

speaker: BS
words: for years here. And the Featherstones - the rest of 'em worked in

speaker: BS
words: the stores and things that was coming in, new.

speaker: SS
words: So they stopped going together, right then?

speaker: BS
words: Yeah, they stopped right there. She wouldn't have nothing to do with

speaker: BS
words: him, 'cause he was down at this sporthouse!! (Chuckles) And he went

speaker: BS
words: down there to quell the trouble. Some of them old 'jacks got down

speaker: BS
words: there and got into trouble and he was helping 'em out!!

speaker: SS
words: Charlotte Bovill?

speaker: BS
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: I heard she was sort of the town nurse and general information source

speaker: SS
words: for quite a few years.

speaker: BS
words: Yes. Yes, she was. Old Hugh used to come up and visit with me and

speaker: BS
words: "we'd go up on the hill here and he'd show me an old mine, used to be

speaker: BS
words: over on the Robbins Creek Divide. He said, he used to go up

speaker: BS
words: and watch him come out with an old wheelbarrow and put the dirt on

speaker: BS
words: the dump. He had a tunnel there. They called it the Black

speaker: SS
words: He, himself, it was his mine? It was his mine, or was he just working

speaker: SS
words: there?

speaker: BS
words: No, he just watched the old fella. Across the canyon, he could see

speaker: BS
words: the old fella come out. I went in there in later years and found the

speaker: BS
words: blacksmith shop and all of his tools and anvil and everything was

speaker: BS
words: right in there. And it's probably in there yet. If they haven't

speaker: BS
words: packed it off.

speaker: SS
words: Well, did Hugh mix with the people, real well?

speaker: BS
words: Oh, yes, yes. He was quite a leader in sports and things that way.

speaker: BS
words: He figured on having a span of horses and take 'em out on sightseeing

speaker: BS
words: here. Hikes and things that way. But things kmda changed.

speaker: SS
words: Pretty fast, huh?

speaker: BS
words: Yes, they changed pretty fast. He'd a been lots better off in the coun

speaker: BS
words: try here; been lots better off if he hadn't been so greedy, charging

speaker: BS
words: the Potlatch such a.exhorbitant price for these meadows and things

speaker: BS
words: here. We'd a had the mill at Elk River instead of being at Lewiston,

speaker: BS
words: why the mill'd a been here yet, today. Because this is al

speaker: BS
words: ways been the center point

speaker: SS
words: Do you think maybe there wouldn't a been that mill at Lewiston, if

speaker: SS
words: they had had the mill here instead?

speaker: BS
words: No. I heard Old Charley Weyerhaeuser and Weyerhaeuser when

speaker: BS
words: they was up talking to— (end cassette A)

speaker: SS
words: Your father?

speaker: BS
words: Yeah. About the mill; putting the mill down at Lewiston. He says,

speaker: BS
words: "We'd a never put the mill at Lewiston, if we had it to do over again."

speaker: BS
words: He says, "We'd a kept the mill at Elk River." Which they would of.

speaker: BS
words: The mill at Lewiston, they done away with the mill at Elk River.

speaker: BS
words: And Elk River's always been the center point.

speaker: SS
words: Why did they decide to go to Lewiston and quit Elk River in the first

speaker: SS
words: place?

speaker: BS
words: Well, they figured they could get all their power and everything on

speaker: BS
words: the river there. Which they could, you know. They put in that dam

speaker: BS
words: and they run the mill right off of the dam. 'Course, they could a

speaker: BS
words: done the same thing at Elk River. That's what they done at Elk River.

speaker: BS
words: They had a good flow of water in there.

speaker: SS
words: I wonder why at the time they thought they'd be better off at Lewiston.

speaker: SS
words: I'd always thought it was just because they had so much of that

speaker: SS
words: timber up on the Clearwater.

speaker: BS
words: No. No. They was bringing the timber— they never started to bring

speaker: BS
words: that out til later years up in there. 'Course, I think when they fore-

speaker: BS
words: 4ha+

speaker: BS
words: see the object of the river being there,.they could flume right down

speaker: BS
words: to Lewiston. Another thing. But you see, all of this country in

speaker: BS
words: here where they've logged for all these years is come into Elk River

speaker: BS
words: anyhow. And, in later years they commenced to flume, every spring

speaker: BS
words: they'd flume down to the mill at Lewiston.

speaker: SS
words: What I'm wondering why there'd be a difference between building the

speaker: SS
words: mill at Elk River or here, as far as what would happen in the long

speaker: SS
words: run.

speaker: BS
words: Well, we was the center,at that time, we was the center of their main

speaker: BS
words: timber. A lot of this timber that they got ahold of in later years,

speaker: BS
words: but their main timber was around Bovill here. Well, they had fifty

speaker: BS
words: years of timber right here without moving at all. And they've still

speaker: BS
words: got timber here.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard that— somebody say that Hugh Bovill didn't like the idea

speaker: SS
words: of the land getting changed around. He was sorry to see the Potlatch

speaker: SS
words: come in, and everything change. Do you think there is any truth to

speaker: SS
words: that?

speaker: BS
words: Well, I don't know about that. But he wasn't very cooperative with

speaker: BS
words: 'em when they come in here and wanted the meadows and stuff. If he'd

speaker: BS
words: a cooperated with 'em they'd a give him his price, but they wouldn't

speaker: BS
words: give him his price. 'Course, that might a turned him, I don't know,

speaker: BS
words: it might a turned him, that he wasn't cooperative with 'em. But, I

speaker: BS
words: don't think it was, because he built that hotel— you see, he only

speaker: BS
words: built half of it then later on he built the other half over to live

speaker: BS
words: in and run the hotel. And then, when the other outfit took over the

speaker: BS
words: hotel, why, they joined the old house and the hotel together; made

speaker: BS
words: one building that stands there yet. They joined 'em together and give

speaker: BS
words: 'em a nice hotel, for that time.

speaker: SS
words: You said to me that the Weyerhaeusers were common people, in a way.

speaker: SS
words: And that made me wonder something about it, how people that got to be

speaker: SS
words: so rich and could still be common people.

speaker: BS
words: Well, they was old stock. The people; they wasn't anything airy about

speaker: BS
words: 'em at all. They started out back there in Minnesota. They'd work

speaker: BS
words: and they'd put every nickle into land. They could buy it for practi

speaker: BS
words: cally nothing. Then they commenced to build up. Now, when they'd

speaker: BS
words: come out and visit here, when they first started out, they'd come out

speaker: BS
words: every year and visit around. Well, they d go right in at the cookshacks

speaker: BS
words: and they'd sit right along — they'd fix 'em a special table,

speaker: BS
words: not special, but the same table, but it would be up at one end. They'd

speaker: BS
words: eat right along with the lumberjacks and talk with 'em. And you take

speaker: BS
words: Old Billy Watts and my father and Old T. Jones, if they was doing

words: My brother, John, over here. He had a team; we had the team together,

speaker: BS
words: And we had tongs and skiddin' riggins.

speaker: SS
words: So you were picking up all that wood?

speaker: BS
words: Yes, we were picking up that dead tamarack and cuttin' it up, and

speaker: BS
words: Damon couldn't get no cash out of it himself, but he'd take it down

speaker: BS
words: to Genesee; he had a farm down Genesee, and he'd trade it to those

speaker: BS
words: farmers for— like I wanted a pig, they'd butcher me a pig. Butcher

speaker: BS
words: it out. I wanted coffee, he'd trade it and get me twenty pounds of

speaker: BS
words: coffee at a time and sugar, but very little— The only cash I got

speaker: BS
words: was a few dollars that Daman himself would give me to buy gas with

speaker: BS
words: for my saw.

speaker: SS
words: So you were getting food, and that was it?

speaker: BS
words: Yes, I was gettin' food. I had a rootcellar out here. I had, oh,

speaker: BS
words: forty, fifty sacks of apples and forty, fifty sacks of potatoes, and

speaker: BS
words: I had a hog and I had lard come up. A hundred pounds of la rd at a

speaker: BS
words: time. The farmers'd render it out you know, and they was tickled to

speaker: BS
words: death to get the wood and I was tickled to death to get the food.

speaker: SS
words: Well, this meat that you had that you were giving people, was that

speaker: SS
words: from your own supply?

speaker: BS
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: SS
words: And you were just giving it away to people that needed it?

speaker: BS
words: Yeah. And they stole, on an average, these people— now there was

speaker: BS
words: one family up at Slabtown there I knew— we'd miss a cow- a steernow

speaker: BS
words: and then. And Dad knew where they was a going alright, but he

speaker: BS
words: says, "They got to have something to eat. You can't hold 'em up."

speaker: BS
words: And he'd never say anything, as long as they didn't steal a whole

speaker: BS
words: bunch.

speaker: SS
words: But you were just giving away meat to people?

speaker: BS
words: Well, yes. There wasn't any money in the country. June Cholsher,

speaker: BS
words: one of the prettiest girls that ever come into this town, beautiful

speaker: BS
words: girl, well educated. Her father was a— well, he never got to be arunnin'

speaker: BS
words: in Boise there, but he worked under the Boise. He was a

speaker: BS
words: judge. And he raised a girl and a boy, and this girl was, oh, she

speaker: BS
words: was a beautiful thing, and she got married, and they never had any

speaker: BS
words: work. Well, I met her one time, and I kinda surmised that they was

speaker: BS
words: pretty hardup, and I asked her, I says, "June, wouldn't you like a

speaker: BS
words: nice roast,nand a couple of steaks?" Well, she says, "I'd be tickled

speaker: BS
words: to death to get 'em. " Well, I took her down a nice big roast and

speaker: BS
words: a couple of steaks, but I had no idea that they was as hardup as they

speaker: BS
words: were. And her husband married a brother of the fellow that wrote

speaker: BS
words: smiles. There in California.

speaker: SS
words: Her husband's sister, you men|?

speaker: BS
words: Her husband— his brother was a piano player there in— and a music

speaker: BS
words: man there in Los Angeles.

speaker: SS
words: And he wrote SMILES?

speaker: BS
words: He wrote SMILES. He made his fortune on that song. And he sent

speaker: BS
words: for June and her father and his brother to come down. Sent 'em the

speaker: BS
words: money to come down on. Well, they went down. June was sick when she

speaker: BS
words: left here; went down. And she went right to the hospital, and the

speaker: BS
words: doctors pronounced it malnutrition. Said that she was so far gone,

speaker: BS
words: starved to death. He pronounced it. He says, "You're starved to death."

speaker: BS
words: He says, "I don't think there's much chance for you." And she died

speaker: BS
words: in just a few days. There, she'd starved to death. Too proud to let

speaker: BS
words: it be known.

speaker: SS
words: What about her husband? Couldn't he get any work for food?

speaker: BS
words: No, there wasn't any work. The Potlatch shut down tight, and even the

speaker: BS
words: stores wouldn't put out no credit to anybody only their old customers

speaker: BS
words: that paid up, and if they didn't pay up, why, they got no more credit.

speaker: BS
words: There are families living right/ here now that owed Old John Groh, he

speaker: BS
words: kept a awful bunch of 'em. He wiped over a hundred thousand dollars

speaker: BS
words: off of the books of debts that was run during the Depression. But he

speaker: BS
words: kepta going and he give lots of 'em credit that never had a nickel or

speaker: BS
words: never had no chance to pay him. That's one thing they can give Old

speaker: BS
words: John Groh credit for. He kept a lot of 'em alive here, but at that

speaker: BS
words: there was lots of families that was too proud to let it be known that

speaker: BS
words: they was hardup that way. That's why Nogle— they was lots of people

speaker: BS
words: awful sore over Nogle, that way. And they had no respect for him, what

speaker: BS
words: ever, because he kept his three sons aworkin' when they was families

speaker: BS
words: right here in town that needed work the very worst way.

speaker: SS
words: What about the government; the WPA and the Public Works Administration.

speaker: BS
words: Well, they never come in until after that.

speaker: SS
words: This was before?

speaker: BS
words: Yeah. Yeah, the WPA never come in til the Depression was practically

speaker: BS
words: over. And this fellow over at Moscow was running it— what is his

speaker: BS
words: name? Run the what-you-call-it office there? Or picture show.

speaker: SS
words: Kenworthy?

speaker: BS
words: Kenworthy was at the head of it. And he was pretty hard boiled, too,

speaker: BS
words: for some of the people to get stuff. They'd go over there- that is,

speaker: BS
words: after it started to get stuff and they had to have certain credentials

speaker: BS
words: to show and everything. I suppose the government held him responsible

speaker: BS
words: too.

speaker: SS
words: Was this Depression or rationing in the war?

speaker: BS
words: The last part of the Depression. And then when Roosevelt took over

speaker: BS
words: and they brought the CCs out, I'll tell you, there was some awful pitiful—

speaker: BS
words: that was in '33, '34, '35, they was some awful sights here that

speaker: BS
words: them kids come out, and they had pants that they could pull clear around

speaker: BS
words: on one side of 'em, and shoes that was that much too long for

speaker: BS
words: 'em. And old ragged shirts.

speaker: BS
words: recorder was turned off at this point.

speaker: SS
words: They did actually go on strike, didn't they around here?

speaker: BS
words: Well, yes, when the Potlatch wouldn't cooperate with 'em and give 'em

speaker: BS
words: a fair shake like cleaning up the camps and everything, they walked

speaker: BS
words: out, and they wouldn't go for those camps. But, the Wobblies got

speaker: BS
words: blamed for, oh, fires, and stuff. They wasn't no more guilty than

speaker: BS
words: you or I were. A fire'd get started and get away from 'em; well, the

speaker: BS
words: Wobblies started it. Well, that was all propaganda. The Wobblies

speaker: BS
words: didn't start a fire, and they was against fire. I've worked with hun

speaker: BS
words: dreds of 'em on these fires and go down the trails w ith dust that

speaker: BS
words: deep, and come down off of the mountain and everytime you'd take a

speaker: BS
words: step it would run, just like water. And you was the dirtiest thing

speaker: BS
words: that you ever seen when you got off a one of 'em. I was pretty lucky,

speaker: BS
words: I was pretty handyaround the kitchen always, and they grabbed me and

speaker: BS
words: put me in the cookhouse. Well, I'd gone like old camp two— the fire

speaker: BS
words: was coming across, at old Camp 2 there, and we made lemonade by the

speaker: BS
words: barrels. We had two big barrels, fifty gallon barrels, and we had

speaker: BS
words: ice, and nice cold drinks, but there was pigs that I knew was in the

speaker: BS
words: water a couple hundred yards up from where we was dipping water out

speaker: BS
words: of what we called the spring. It didn't kill anybody but, at the same

speaker: BS
words: time, it wasn't good. No, I'll tell you these camps in the early days

speaker: BS
words: had everything the Wobblies throwed at 'em, coming to 'em. Because

speaker: BS
words: they were filthy. You'd go into old Bob Jones's camp and those pigsy

speaker: BS
words: and the sun  . been shining; it was hot anyhow, and t he steam off a

speaker: BS
words: them would come up and you'd throw up! An outsider'd c ome in- it'd

speaker: BS
words: make you almost throw up; gag you that much. Well, you know how pigs

speaker: BS
words: are.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: BS
words: They're terrible.

speaker: SS
words: I never had to live in with them.

speaker: BS
words: No, the Wobblies got blamed for lots of stuff. Old Pat Malone— some

speaker: BS
words: of the fellows'd get drunk, "The Wobblies-" And old Summerfields'd

speaker: BS
words: come over and they'd round up a bunch of 'em that had b een drinking,

speaker: BS
words: having a good time and take 'em over and throw 'em in the bullpen over

speaker: BS
words: there at Moscow. (Chuckles) All uncalled for, at all !

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember any of the— now I heard that— I've heard people say

speaker: SS
words: that they had some philosophy about— sure, they wanted better conditions-

speaker: SS
words: but I've heard that they believed in ¥workers having power.

speaker: BS
words: No, huh-uh.

speaker: SS
words: Not really true.

speaker: BS
words: No. Now, there was this old fellow I was telling you about, I talked

speaker: BS
words: lots with him. He says, "I'll tell you, Sonny," he says, "I may never

speaker: BS
words: see it, but the time's acomin' when they'll clean these camps up."

speaker: BS
words: And, he says, "The Wobblies- the working man," he didn't call 'em Wob

speaker: BS
words: blies, he says, "the working man is the only man that can make 'em

speaker: BS
words: clean up. And, you' 11 see the day when these camps are cleaned up."

speaker: BS
words: Well, he was right.

speaker: SS
words: I should get going and I'll come back and finish up.
